summary: Soaring Jerry solos & classic NFA→Scarlet sequence
blurb: The setlist delivers strong highlights from Never Trust A Woman and Stagger Lee to a soaring Not Fade Away → Scarlet Begonias > Fire on the Mountain transition. Tight rhythm work lifts the second‑set, ending with a vibrant Black Muddy River encore.
review:
September 18 1988 at Madison Square Garden finds the Dead in fine late‑80s form. Jerry’s guitar rides high on “magic carpet” flights throughout, especially during the iconic Not Fade Away → Scarlet Begonias > Fire on the Mountain chain that propels the second set. Brent adds a bright vocal edge and even a nostalgic high‑pitched tone, while Bill’s drumming carries a crisp “Man Smart” feel that locks the groove. The band shines on “Never Trust A Woman,” “Stagger Lee,” and a soulful “Wharf Rat,” wrapping up with an uplifting “Black Muddy River.” Though Bob’s mic slipped briefly on “Throwing Stones,” his energy remains infectious, and the overall performance feels tight and spirited.
The soundboard recording (gd88-09-18.sbd.eD.12746.sbeok.shnf) captures this energy with exceptional clarity—crisp, balanced, and free of drop‑outs—making it the definitive source for experiencing the night’s highlights.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All three recordings are marked positive, repeatedly praising the same standout moments (NFA→Scarlet, soaring Jerry solos, tight rhythm section). Multiple sources highlight the same songs and note excellent playing throughout, indicating strong consensus of a high‑quality performance.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard source described as 'crispy' and clear, capturing the full band dynamics and all highlighted songs without drop‑outs.

key_highlights: Iconic Not Fade Away → Scarlet Begonias > Fire on the Mountain segue lifts second set momentum.
Jerry’s soaring guitar flights throughout the night, especially during jams.
Tight rhythm section with Bill's crisp drumming and Brent’s strong vocal contributions.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar soars with 'magic carpet' flights, delivering standout solos especially during the NFA→Scarlet transition.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocals suffered a brief mic drop on “Throwing Stones,” but his energy and stage presence remain strong throughout.
band_performance.Brent: Provides solid vocal contributions; one reviewer notes a high‑pitched tone reminiscent of early ’80s style, adding brightness to the mix.
band_performance.Billy: Drumming is tight and rhythmic, with Bill’s beats likened to the ‘Man Smart’ feel, anchoring the band’s groove.
